proposed_hypotheses.text: Among the 125 candidate features, at least one ordinal performance-status-like variable (feature_057, with levels 0/1/2 and prevalence 39%/49%/12%) is inversely associated with objective_response: response rates decrease monotonically as feature_057 increases.
Higher values of the continuous variable feature_011 (range 0-23.6) are associated with lower probability of objective_response.
Higher values of the continuous variable feature_006 (range 20-100, plausibly a clinical performance/index measure) are associated with lower probability of objective_response.

analyses.result_summary: Response rates by feature_057 level: 0=21.3% (n=17,592), 1=15.5% (n=24,971), 2=11.2% (n=7,437). Strict monotonic decrease. Chi-square p=5.5e-98. Logistic regression treating feature_057 as linear: beta=-0.384 per level, OR=0.681, p=1.8e-97.

Mean feature_011 in responders=3.38 vs non-responders=3.95 (Welch t-test p=7.2e-39). Logistic regression on standardized feature_011: beta=-0.155 per SD, OR=0.86 per SD, p=7.4e-35. Quartile dose-response: response rates 18.6% (Q1) -> 16.8% (Q2) -> 13.6% (Q3) -> Q4 collapses with Q3 due to heavy mass at the lower end.

analyses.code: ttest_ind on feature_006 by outcome; logistic regression on z-scored feature_006
analyses.result_summary: Mean feature_006 in responders=58.2 vs non-responders=60.3 (Welch t-test p=9.5e-20). Logistic regression on standardized feature_006: beta=-0.109 per SD, OR=0.90 per SD, p=1.2e-19. Quartile dose-response is monotonic: 19.0% -> 17.5% -> 16.5% -> 14.7%.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Patients with feature_035=1 (a rare binary marker, prevalence 7.3%) have a higher probability of objective_response than patients with feature_035=0.
feature_092 (continuous, range 0.5-500, plausibly a high-skew lab value) is inversely associated with objective_response.
feature_063 (continuous, wide-range lab) is inversely associated with objective_response.
feature_099 (continuous, range 1.7-5.5) is positively associated with objective_response.

analyses.result_summary: Response rate is 22.1% when feature_035=1 (n=3,624) vs 16.5% when feature_035=0 (n=46,376). Logistic OR=1.44 (beta=+0.364), p=4.1e-18.
Quartile rates: 18.0% -> 17.4% -> 16.6% -> 15.6% (monotonic). Logistic per SD: beta=-0.056, OR=0.95, p=1.9e-05.
Q4 of feature_063 has notably lower response (15.6%) than Q1-Q3 (~17.3%). Logistic per SD: beta=-0.064, OR=0.94, p=2.8e-06.
Quartile rates increase: 15.8% -> 16.8% -> 17.6% -> 17.8%. Logistic per SD: beta=+0.058, OR=1.06, p=1.4e-06. Effect direction confirmed positive.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Objective response rates differ across categories of feature_005 (race: white, black, hispanic, asian, other), with white patients having higher response than non-white patients.
Objective response rates differ across categories of feature_087 (insurance: medicaid, medicare, private, uninsured); uninsured patients have lower response than privately insured patients.
Patient age (feature_078, range 18-92, mean 68) is inversely associated with objective_response.

analyses.result_summary: Response rates: white 17.3%, hispanic 16.7%, asian 16.3%, black 15.7%, other 14.7%. Overall chi-square p=0.0096. In a logistic model with 'asian' as reference, none of the individual race coefficients reach p<0.05 (e.g., white: beta=+0.068, p=0.19; black: beta=-0.044, p=0.48); the unadjusted association is small (max absolute difference 2.6 percentage points) and driven mainly by a slight 'white > all others' pattern.
Response rates: medicare 17.2%, medicaid 16.9%, private 16.7%, uninsured 16.6%. Chi-square p=0.78. No detectable disparity by insurance category.

analyses.code: logistic regression of objective_response on z-scored feature_078
analyses.result_summary: Logistic per SD of age: beta=+0.004, OR=1.00, p=0.72. No detectable main effect of feature_078 on response.

proposed_hypotheses.text: In a multivariable logistic model that includes feature_057, feature_011, feature_006, feature_063, feature_092, feature_099, feature_035, feature_078, race (feature_005), and insurance (feature_087), the apparent univariate race association (white > non-white) attenuates and becomes non-significant, indicating that race-level differences in response are explained by clinical/lab features rather than race itself.

After full clinical adjustment, the strongest independent predictors of response remain feature_057 (negative), feature_035 (positive), feature_011 (negative), feature_006 (negative), feature_099 (positive), and feature_063 (negative).

analyses.code: smf.logit('objective_response ~ C(feature_057) + feature_011 + feature_006 + feature_092 + feature_063 + feature_099 + feature_035 + feature_078 + C(feature_005) + C(feature_087)', df).fit()
analyses.result_summary: After adjustment, race coefficients all become non-significant (white vs asian beta=+0.080, p=0.13; black beta=-0.029, p=0.64; hispanic beta=+0.045, p=0.45; other beta=-0.118, p=0.25). Insurance coefficients also non-significant (all p>0.5). The univariate race signal is fully explained by adjustment; the data do not support a residual race-based disparity in objective response.

Adjusted log-odds (z values): feature_057 level 1 vs 0: beta=-0.387 (z=-15.1, p<1e-50); level 2 vs 0: beta=-0.776 (z=-18.7, p<1e-77); feature_035: beta=+0.361 (z=8.5, p=1.5e-17); feature_011 (raw): beta=-0.040 per unit (z=-12.3, p<1e-30); feature_006: beta=-0.006 per unit (z=-9.1, p<1e-19); feature_099: beta=+0.122 per unit (z=4.9, p=4.3e-07); feature_063: beta=-0.007 per unit (z=-4.8, p=1.0e-06); feature_092: beta=-0.002 per unit (z=-4.4, p=1.1e-05). feature_078 (age): beta=+0.0003, p=0.78 — null. All hypothesized predictors retained their direction and statistical significance under joint adjustment.

proposed_hypotheses.text: The positive effect of feature_035=1 on objective_response is heterogeneous across feature_057 strata, with a larger absolute response-rate gain in patients with feature_057=0 (better performance) than in feature_057=2.
The effect of feature_035=1 on response is modified by age (elderly = age>=75): the OR is smaller in elderly patients than in younger patients.
The effect of feature_035=1 on response is heterogeneous across race (feature_005): the OR varies by race.

analyses.result_summary: Stratified rates: feature_057=0: 20.8% (035=0) vs 27.0% (035=1), diff +6.2 pp; feature_057=1: 15.1% vs 20.5%, diff +5.4 pp; feature_057=2: 10.9% vs 15.3%, diff +4.4 pp. The two interaction coefficients (level 1: +0.028, p=0.75; level 2: +0.053, p=0.71) are NOT significant. On the log-odds scale the OR for feature_035 is essentially constant across feature_057 levels; the absolute risk difference shrinks only because baseline rates fall. Hypothesis of effect-modification on the multiplicative scale is REFUTED.

feature_035 effect: in younger (<75) OR=1.49, p=8.8e-17; in elderly (>=75) OR=1.29, p=3.0e-03. Interaction term beta=-0.151, p=0.122 — directionally consistent with a smaller effect in elderly but NOT statistically significant.

analyses.code: LR test of feature_035 * C(feature_005) vs additive model
analyses.result_summary: Stratified ORs for feature_035: white 1.50 (p=2.6e-15), hispanic 1.31 (p=0.012), black 1.33 (p=0.025), asian 1.31 (p=0.13), other 1.47 (p=0.20). Likelihood-ratio test for interaction: LR=2.06 on 4 df, p=0.73. Hypothesis REFUTED — no race-based effect modification.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Among 80 binary features (prevalence 2-98%), at least one shows a strong multiplicative interaction with feature_035 such that feature_035=1 gives a much larger OR for response when that other feature equals 1 than when it equals 0.

analyses.code: for each binary col c: LR test comparing 'objective_response ~ feature_035 * c' vs 'feature_035 + c'
analyses.result_summary: Two strong interactions found (in the 72 binary features tested after prevalence filter): (a) feature_035 x feature_027: LR=139.8 on 1 df, p<1e-30, interaction coef +1.046 (subgroup table: feature_027=1 & feature_035=1 -> 35.2% response vs 16.0% in feature_027=1 & feature_035=0); (b) feature_035 x feature_014: LR=74.3 on 1 df, p<1e-15, interaction coef +0.730 (feature_014=1 & feature_035=1 -> 29.8% vs 16.4%). A weaker third candidate (feature_035 x feature_058) was significant (p=0.0014) but with a small subgroup. The hypothesis that some binary features modify the feature_035 effect is strongly supported.

proposed_hypotheses.text: feature_027 (binary, prevalence 30%) has minimal main effect on response after controlling for feature_035 because its apparent effect operates almost entirely through interaction with feature_035.
feature_014 (binary, prevalence 40%) similarly has minimal main effect once interaction with feature_035 is modeled.
The two two-way interactions (feature_035 x feature_027 and feature_035 x feature_014) are not redundant: both terms remain significant when included in the same model.

analyses.code: smf.logit('objective_response ~ feature_035 * feature_027 + feature_035 * feature_014', df).fit()
analyses.result_summary: Joint model: feature_035 main effect alone (when 027=014=0) shifts to beta=-0.329 (p=4e-06) — i.e., when neither modifier is present, feature_035=1 actually slightly LOWERS the odds of response. Main effect of feature_027 = -0.055 (p=0.047), feature_014 = -0.013 (p=0.62). Both interaction terms are highly significant: feature_035:feature_027 = +1.058 (z=11.9, p<1e-30); feature_035:feature_014 = +0.746 (z=8.6, p<1e-15). Hypotheses h17, h18, h19 supported.

proposed_hypotheses.text: There is a synergistic three-way interaction between feature_035, feature_027, and feature_014 such that the response rate is dramatically higher when all three equal 1 than would be predicted from main effects plus pairwise interactions alone.

analyses.result_summary: 8-cell mean response rates: (027=0, 014=0, 035=0) 16.9%; (035=1) 17.3%; (014=1, 035=0) 16.5%; (014=1, 035=1) 16.1%; (027=1, 014=0, 035=0) 15.9%; (027=1, 014=0, 035=1) 16.8%; (027=1, 014=1, 035=0) 16.1%; (027=1, 014=1, 035=1) 62.1% (n=427). Three-way interaction coefficient = +2.144, z=11.2, p<1e-28. All lower-order interactions involving feature_035 collapse toward zero in the saturated model. The hypothesized synergistic triple interaction is strongly supported and is the dominant driver of the apparent two-way effects from earlier iterations.

proposed_hypotheses.text: After adjusting for the triple interaction (feature_035 x feature_027 x feature_014) and for the main clinical predictors (feature_057, feature_011, feature_006, feature_092, feature_063, feature_099, feature_078), race (feature_005) and insurance (feature_087) remain independently NON-significant, i.e., demographic disparity is not present in this cohort's response outcome after appropriate clinical adjustment.
feature_093 (binary, prevalence 8%) and feature_121 (binary, prevalence 8%) are independently associated with response after full adjustment (positive and negative direction respectively).

analyses.code: Final logistic with triple interaction + clinical + demographics
analyses.result_summary: Full-model demographic coefficients (asian = ref): white +0.075 (p=0.15), black -0.037 (p=0.56), hispanic +0.039 (p=0.51), other -0.110 (p=0.29). Insurance (medicaid = ref): medicare +0.022 (p=0.55), private -0.018 (p=0.64), uninsured -0.010 (p=0.89). All clinical predictors retain their previously established direction and significance. No residual race or insurance disparity is detectable in objective_response after clinical adjustment. Hypothesis supported.

analyses.code: Final multivariable logistic model coefficients for feature_093 and feature_121
analyses.result_summary: feature_093: adjusted beta=+0.131 (z=3.04, p=0.0024) - higher response when feature_093=1. feature_121: adjusted beta=-0.088 (z=-1.95, p=0.052) - borderline negative association, not quite reaching significance. h22 supported for feature_093 and equivocal for feature_121.

proposed_hypotheses.text: Patients with the joint phenotype (feature_027=1 AND feature_014=1 AND feature_035=1) constitute a small subpopulation (<1% of cohort) whose objective_response rate (>=50%) is several-fold higher than that of the rest of the cohort, consistent with a biomarker-defined subgroup that is highly responsive to a specific therapy or vice versa.

Outside the (feature_027=1, feature_014=1, feature_035=1) subgroup, feature_035 has effectively no main effect on response, so the apparent univariate OR=1.44 for feature_035 (Iteration 2) is entirely driven by this small high-response subgroup.

analyses.result_summary: Triple-positive subgroup: n=427 (0.85% of 50,000), response rate = 62.1%. Rest of cohort: n=49,573, response rate = 16.5%. Risk difference = +45.6 pp; OR (univariate) = 8.30, p<1e-100. Hypothesis strongly supported.

Restricting to patients without the (feature_027=1 AND feature_014=1) genotype combination: feature_035 main-effect beta=-0.04, OR=0.96, p>0.4. Outside the modifier-positive subgroup, feature_035 carries no detectable response benefit. The cohort-wide OR=1.44 for feature_035 is dominated by the 427-patient triple-positive cell. Hypothesis supported.
